speaker: interviewer
text: You were just expressing what your children mean to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope that I have been able to-- to transmit some of the overwhelming meaning that my family represents to me and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how they were the most important instrument of my surviving my survival. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That, and as I have said before, my work, which in whatever form it may have taken-- and sometimes it took pretty odd forms-- with always directed that the renewal of the Jewish people and-- and helping to assure its continuity. Which, as I have said before also, was to me the be-all and end-all of the survival. And-- and the survival itself, which to me represents victory over that unspeakable and indescribable evil that Nazism was, not only to-- to the Jews but to all of mankind, whether or not portions of mankind recognized it for what it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and which has really enabled me, surrounded by-- by the love of family and the support of-- of friends-- and some of them very particular and very precious friends, who probably, certainly in my mind, represent the best of humanity and the best of America. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- I have lived what I consider a comfortable and a pleasant and-- and, I hope, a constructive life, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I think is also what has enabled me, I hope, to on the one hand transmit [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my heritage, my beliefs, my values to my children, in a manner that broadened their-- their emotional and-- and mental horizons, without damaging them. I believe my children [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are probably [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] less affected than any other children of survivors that I know. When I say less affected, I mean less deleteriously affected than anyone else I know or have heard of. My children have the knowledge [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that their father [SOB] had [SOBBING] gone through the hell of the Holocaust [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] but came out whole, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and-- [SOB] as a complete human being, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] without [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] losing his faith in the potential of man, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] however imperfect he may be, and in the possibility of improvement of man and his works, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] which I think is enough for one life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip, I want to thank you for sharing all of your memories with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my obligation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I hope that it will contribute to the awareness of many other people. I don't mean only my testimony, and I don't know whether that's even the right phrase for it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that it somehow will work its way, the story of the Holocaust-- to the extent that it-- it can be told at all-- work its way through the human consciousness and make man somewhat better and closer to the potential that he has.

speaker: interviewer
text: It has been an honor to be here with you today. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture of the main synagogue in Kisvarda, at the very start of Star Street, that I have described and which I called the shul. This, of course, was in its-- in its glory days. Today that fence that is around it is-- is gone. And the very colorful windows have become less colorful. As I understand it and as I have seen it, on one of the two trips that I have taken to-- back to Kisvarda, it is now some kind of an anthropological institution, where exhibits and lectures are being held. This is the first and second inside page of my Hungarian passport, obtained in Bern, in Switzerland, in a very interesting and adventurous way, which would take too long to describe. But the [? upwards ?] of it, it's more visible that it is a-- a passport issued by a representative of an non-existent government and state. It is issued sometime in September of 1945, as a-- a passport of the royal Hungarian government, which of course had long since ceased to exist. And there are other peculiarities in this passport, again, that would take very long to-- to describe, which I think make it quite unique. This is a certificate issued by the military governor at Feldafing, in the DP camp, requesting assistance to me, who is on his way to be repatriated to the United States, sometime-- I don't remember now the exact date-- in 1945. This is a picture of me on the Liberty ship, on the way to the United States, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which is also a very complex story for-- all together, down to the pants I wear or the jacket that I have on. But you can see that I was already in a pretty sound condition, it was-- even though it was only about four or five months after the liberation. This is a picture of my father and mother, taken out of their joint passport [? that ?] they had at one time. I would say that my father couldn't be more than maybe 40, 42 there. And my mother must have been in-- 38 to 39, or something of that kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this would have been approximately in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: This had to be sometime in '39, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: '38, before your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: '36, something like that, yeah. This is a picture of the family that we had sent to my father in the United States. So it had to be after '39, possibly in-- in the early '40s. And it shows my mother, obviously, in the bottom row, with my youngest sister, Helen. And on the upper row, left to right, is Berta, Izabella, and myself, my oldest sister, Jolan, and my sister Regina. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This should prove to anyone who-- who saw it that I was telling the truth. It's a-- a command car that was given to me by the military authorities for my, so to say, official use in Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a letter, written at the behest of my father-- because my father couldn't write English like that-- apparently sometime early June of 1945, shortly after my liberation. It's addressed to Private Berent, who I discovered later was the American soldier who I buttonholed minutes after our liberation and asked him to advise my father, at his sister's address in Brooklyn, that I was alive. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: K. Shall we start with this one? You were pretty young here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, do you want to show all of those things?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, yeah, we'll show a lot of them. We don't have to show all of them. But there were a lot--

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I think maybe you should give a teeny introduction that these pictures were taken--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in conjunction--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me, just let me, I-- I'll do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not just, you know, personal pictures and then, boom. [INAUDIBLE] out of the [? clear blue. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so Philip will introduce this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture and the next few pictures that you will be showing are actually a kind of illustration of some of the work that I have done and the encounters I have had with various officials in various parts of the world. And most particularly in Israel, in connection with the work that I had been doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you identify who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this particular picture, there is Shimon Peres, who looks his age, which is exactly my age. And I think we both look quite young there. And there are-- there are two members of the B'nai B'rith Israel commission-- in fact, the chairman, Morris Alexander, and the co-chairman, Meyer Halperin, who are sitting with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken in approximately--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in the Defense Department Office of Shimon Peres, when he was Director General of the Ministry of Defense, sometime probably in '57, '58. This is a picture of a conversation with Senator Hubert Humphrey, when he was getting ready to run for president. This is a picture with the former Prime Minister David Ben-Gurion, at his home in stable care, with Mr. Label Katz, who was then international president of the B'nai B'rith. As you can see, we have-- have quite a lively debate with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the benefit of the viewers, which--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: --one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm obviously the one looking over the head of Mr. Katz-- no-- no relation, by the way. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is a picture of a-- an audience with Pope Paul VI, at a crucial moment in the process of the Second Vatican Council, where the so-called Jewish constitution was being debated and was in some trouble. And we were granted this audience, where we had a chance to-- to discuss with the Pope our problems with the so-called Jewish constitution. And hopefully, it was of some-- some help. It was part of a long and convoluted process of Jewish involvement with the Vatican Council II. And on the picture, from left to right, is Saul Joftes, who was then director of the International department of B'nai B'rith; and Label Katz, the president; Pope Paul VI; and-- let's see-- Maurice Bisgyer, who was then executive vice president of B'nai B'rith; and Bernard Simon, who was the director of communications; and yours truly. Please note the little red box, which contains a special medal that the pope had given us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been in '62, '63. I think Vatican Council II was around that time. I really don't remember anymore. But I think it must have been in '62, '63. This is a picture of me at the time when I served as executive vice president of the America-Israel Cultural Foundation. And we were opening an exhibit in-- in our gallery of some Israel artwork. And next to me is Abie Nathan, the famous peacenik of Israel, and Teddy Kollek, who then was I think still director general of the prime minister's office. At any rate, he wasn't mayor yet at the time. This is a picture with Senator Edward Kennedy. And again, you can see that he was somewhat younger and even smaller in those days. This actually was already taken in Canada. If I recall correctly, he came here at my invitation, to speak to the Canadian Associates of Ben-Gurion University. This is a picture at some synagogue in Montreal where Elie Wiesel was speaking. I don't know who the other people are who are in the picture, besides Elie and myself. As my wife told you off camera, we-- we met-- I think it must have been in 1957-- and have remained friends ever since then. Although, of course, because of our respective spheres and-- and-- and preoccupations, we don't meet too frequently. But whenever we meet, we are long-lost friends. This is a picture of myself with former prime minister Levi Eshkol. This is a picture of me translating from the Yiddish Dr. Moses Rosen, who was chief rabbi of Romania all through the post-war years, until just about a few years ago and whom we brought out first-- for the first time to the United States and attempted to assist him and-- and through him the Jewish community of Romania for many, many years. This is a picture of myself looking up at a-- a commemorative plaque in Kiev of a building. And the plaque states that this is the place where the famous Ukrainian writer lived for-- in a certain-- for a certain period of time. It's detailed on the picture, the-- the years. And of course, it is Sholem Aleichem. And this was taken in 1960, at the time when we, with the-- the sponsorship of the government of the United States and particularly Mrs. Roosevelt, we managed to be-- lead a delegation for the first time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certainly in the post-war years-- to survey Jewish life in the Soviet Union. This is a picture with Judge Musmanno, who was one of the American judges on the Nuremberg Trials, and Judith and Gideon Hausner. Mr. Hausner was the prosecutor of Eichmann, just I would say about a year before that. And he was visiting with us-- that is, with B'nai B'rith-- at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you see what the date is on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is a picture of the president or the former president of Israel, with whom I had many years of connection. Prior to that, he in fact was the-- the chairman of the Israel Committee of the America-Israel Cultural Foundation. And the-- on the left is Gordon Echenberg, a Montreal attorney who was the first national president of the Canadian Associates of Ben-Gurion University-- and Stephen-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't remember his name-- who was at one time the leader of the NDP and also [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an ambassador, representing Canada at the UN.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephen Lewis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephen Lewis. [INAUDIBLE] his name [INAUDIBLE]. This is a picture in the office of former Minister of Agriculture of Canada-- and for the life of me, I can't remember his name-- and Don [? Johnson, ?] who was at that time-- what is that called, exchequer or something like that-- in the Trudeau government, myself, and Mr. [? Mel ?] [? Zwaig, ?] who [? was in ?] the National President of Canadian Associates. [BACKGROUND NOISE] As an indication of the versatility of a-- the life of a Jewish communal servant, this was the occasion of a presentation of a check of $60,000 from the Ministry of Agriculture to help in-- in the Freedom from Hunger conference that I had organized under the auspices of the Canadian Associates of Ben-Gurion University, in 1985. I suppose there can be no more appropriate way to end this rather long session than by showing you my-- my fundraising pamphlet on my retirement, after almost 50 years of Jewish communal service, as executive director of the Canadian Associates of Ben-Gurion University, at which time the organization has raised a sizable sum of money for a student cultural program at Ben-Gurion University in my wife's and my name.
